IndicatorsBornDiedLife
Puritan · New England1629–1640East Anglia → Massachusetts Bay
Benjamin FessendenHalf 5th cousin 9× removed Puritan1629–1640 — — Kent, England (family origin)17th c. Massachusetts—
  • Colonial tavern keeper; among the earliest traceable American ancestors
  • Family crossed from Kent in the Great Migration (1629–1640), part of ~20,000 Puritans seeking freedom of worship
  • His tavern served a young Massachusetts Bay community
Martha Carrier (Allen)Half 4th cousin 9× removed Puritan1629–1640 Salem Trials — Andover, Massachusettsc. 1650 Salem, Massachusetts (executed)19 Aug 1692
  • Accused of witchcraft in the Salem hysteria after being blamed for Andover's 1690 smallpox outbreak
  • Defiant at trial — told the magistrates she was 'wronged'; Cotton Mather branded her 'Queen of Hell'
  • Hanged on Gallows Hill; formally exonerated by Massachusetts in 2001
Rufus KingHalf 5th cousin 7× removed Puritan1629–1640 Founder Founding era Scarborough, Mass. (now Maine)24 Mar 1755 Jamaica, New York29 Apr 1827
  • Founding Father and signer of the U.S. Constitution
  • Harvard 1777; militia officer in the Revolution, then lawyer and Massachusetts delegate
  • Later U.S. Senator for New York, twice Minister to Britain, and a lifelong opponent of slavery
Eli WhitneyHalf 7th cousin 5× removed Puritan1629–1640 — — Westborough, Massachusetts8 Dec 1765 New Haven, Connecticut8 Jan 1825
  • Inventor of the cotton gin (1793) and father of American mass production
  • Ran a nail-making operation as a boy during the Revolution; Yale 1792
  • Pioneered interchangeable parts manufacturing for U.S. Army muskets
Emma WillardHalf 5th cousin 7× removed Puritan1629–1640 — — Berlin, Connecticut23 Feb 1787 Troy, New York15 Apr 1870
  • Pioneer of women's higher education in America
  • Sixteenth of seventeen children; teaching at 17, running a school by 20
  • Founded the Troy Female Seminary (1821), the first school offering women a college-level education
Louisa C. Johnson AdamsHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Puritan1629–1640 Founder era · First Lady — London, England12 Feb 1775 Washington, D.C.15 May 1852
  • First Lady 1825–1829; the only First Lady born outside the United States
  • Married John Quincy Adams in London, 1797; accompanied him on diplomatic postings across Europe
  • Made a celebrated 40-day winter journey from St Petersburg to Paris in 1815
Mary Newbury AdamsHalf 8th cousin 3× removed Puritan1629–1640 — — Peru, Indiana1837 Dubuque, Iowa1901
  • Iowa suffragist, education reformer, and frontier-Midwest cultural figure
  • Raised in an abolitionist household that moved between congregations willing to hear antislavery preaching
  • Championed women's intellectual societies across the Midwest
Joseph K. F. MansfieldHalf 7th cousin 5× removed Puritan1629–1640 Civil War (Union) Union New Haven, Connecticut22 Dec 1803 Antietam, Maryland (KIA)18 Sep 1862
  • Union major general; graduated 2nd in his West Point class of 1822
  • Four decades an army engineer; breveted for gallantry at Monterrey and Buena Vista in Mexico
  • Given XII Corps two days before Antietam; mortally wounded leading it into the East Woods
Nathaniel LyonHalf 5th cousin 7× removed Puritan1629–1640 Civil War (Union) Union Ashford, Connecticut14 Jul 1818 Wilson's Creek, Missouri (KIA)10 Aug 1861
  • First Union general killed in the Civil War
  • West Point 1841; veteran of the Seminole and Mexican wars
  • His bold action at St Louis is credited with keeping Missouri in the Union
George StonemanHalf 7th cousin 5× removed Puritan1629–1640 Civil War (Union) Union Busti, New York22 Aug 1822 Buffalo, New York5 Sep 1894
  • Union cavalry general; West Point roommate of the future Stonewall Jackson
  • Refused to surrender Fort Brown to the Confederacy in 1861 and escaped north with his troops
  • Led the Army of the Potomac's Cavalry Corps; later Governor of California
Halbert E. PaineHalf 8th cousin 3× removed Puritan1629–1640 Civil War (Union) Union Chardon, Ohio4 Feb 1826 Washington, D.C.14 Apr 1905
  • Union brigadier general who twice refused orders he judged immoral
  • Lost a leg at Port Hudson, 1863
  • Three-term Wisconsin Congressman and later U.S. Commissioner of Patents
Edward P. ChapinHalf 6th cousin 6× removed Puritan1629–1640 Civil War (Union) Union Waterloo, New York1831 Port Hudson, Louisiana (KIA)27 May 1863
  • Union brigadier general, descended from Deacon Samuel Chapin, a founder of Springfield, Mass.
  • Lawyer in Rochester; enlisted as a captain in the 44th New York, 1861
  • Killed at 31 leading the assault on Port Hudson — among the first battles where Black troops fought alongside white soldiers
Quaker · Delaware Valley1675–1725English Midlands → Pennsylvania
Benjamin RushHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Quaker1675–1725 Founder (signer) Founding era Byberry, Pennsylvania4 Jan 1746 Philadelphia, Pennsylvania19 Apr 1813
  • Founding Father; the only physician to sign the Declaration of Independence
  • Surgeon General of the Continental Army's Middle Department; taught ~3,000 medical students
  • Mentor to Thomas Paine; later reconciled Adams and Jefferson by letter
Betsy RossHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Quaker1675–1725 Founder Founding era Philadelphia, Pennsylvania1 Jan 1752 Philadelphia, Pennsylvania30 Jan 1836
  • Upholsterer and Revolutionary War flag-maker, credited with the first Stars and Stripes
  • Eighth of seventeen children in a Quaker family; expelled from meeting for marrying outside the faith
  • Ran her upholstery business for over 50 years, widowed three times
Robert K. ScottHalf 8th cousin 3× removed Quaker1675–1725 Civil War (Union) Union Armstrong County, Pennsylvania8 Jul 1826 Napoleon, Ohio12 Aug 1900
  • Union brigadier general and Reconstruction Governor of South Carolina (1868–1872)
  • Physician and Gold Rush forty-niner before the war
  • Grandfather fought in the Revolution, father in the War of 1812
Cavalier · Tidewater1642–1675South & West of England → Virginia and Maryland
Thomas StoneHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Cavalier1642–1675 Founder (signer) Founding era Poynton Manor, Charles Co., Maryland1743 Alexandria, Virginia5 Oct 1787
  • Signer of the Declaration of Independence for Maryland
  • Descended from William Stone, Chesapeake settler of the 1640s and third Proprietary Governor of Maryland
  • Helped draft the Articles of Confederation; died at 44, reportedly of grief, four months after his wife Margaret
James MadisonHalf 7th cousin 5× removed Cavalier1642–1675 Founder · 4th President U.S. President Port Conway, Virginia16 Mar 1751 Montpelier, Virginia28 Jun 1836
  • 4th President (1809–1817) and 'Father of the Constitution'
  • Co-author of the Federalist Papers; drafted the Bill of Rights
  • As Secretary of State negotiated the Louisiana Purchase; last surviving signer of the Constitution
Richard L. PageHalf 7th cousin 4× removed Cavalier1642–1675 Civil War (Confederate) Confederacy Clarke County, Virginia20 Dec 1807 Blue Ridge Summit, Pennsylvania9 Aug 1901
  • Confederate brigadier general and maternal cousin of Robert E. Lee
  • 37 years a U.S. Navy officer before the war
  • Commanded Fort Morgan in the Battle of Mobile Bay, 1864
Samuel CooperHalf 5th cousin 8× removed Cavalier1642–1675 Civil War (Confederate) Confederacy New Hackensack, New York12 Jun 1798 Alexandria, Virginia3 Dec 1876
  • Highest-ranking general in the Confederate Army — senior even to Robert E. Lee
  • Entered West Point at 15; career staff officer and U.S. Adjutant General
  • Preserved the Confederate war records at the surrender, a priceless gift to history
Scots-Irish · Backcountry1717–1775Ulster & the Borders → the Appalachian frontier
Joseph HewesHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Founder (signer) Founding era Kingston, New Jersey23 Jan 1730 Philadelphia, Pennsylvania10 Nov 1779
  • Signer of the Declaration of Independence for North Carolina
  • Edenton merchant with a fleet of five ships
  • Effectively the first head of the American Navy; secured John Paul Jones his commission
James K. PolkHalf 7th cousin Scots-Irish1717–1775 11th President U.S. President Pineville, North Carolina2 Nov 1795 Nashville, Tennessee15 Jun 1849
  • 11th President (1845–1849), 'Young Hickory', protégé of Andrew Jackson
  • Only person to serve as Speaker of the House and President
  • Achieved every major goal of his single term — Oregon, California, the Southwest — then died 103 days after leaving office
Abraham BaldwinHalf 4th cousin 8× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Founder (signer) Founding era North Guilford, Connecticut22 Nov 1754 Washington, D.C.4 Mar 1807
  • Founding Father and signer of the U.S. Constitution for Georgia
  • Yale-trained minister and Continental Army chaplain
  • Founded the University of Georgia, the first state-chartered university in America
Nancy HartHalf 6th cousin 7× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Founder era (Revolutionary heroine) Founding era Yadkin Valley, North Carolinac. 1735 Henderson County, Kentuckyc. 1830
  • Revolutionary War folk hero of the Georgia backcountry
  • Tradition: six feet tall, red-haired, a crack shot — captured a squad of Loyalist soldiers at her cabin
  • Georgia's Hart County is the only one in the state named for a woman
Thomas J. ‘Stonewall’ JacksonHalf 8th cousin 3× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Civil War (Confederate) Confederacy Clarksburg, Virginia (now W.Va.)21 Jan 1824 Guinea Station, Virginia10 May 1863
  • Confederate lieutenant general; earned his name at First Bull Run — 'There stands Jackson like a stone wall!'
  • Orphaned young; West Point 1846; VMI professor
  • Accidentally shot by his own men at Chancellorsville; Lee mourned losing 'his right arm'
David CrockettHalf 7th cousin 4× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Alamo defender Texas / Alamo Greene County, Tennessee17 Aug 1786 The Alamo, San Antonio, Texas6 Mar 1836
  • Frontiersman, three-term Congressman, 'King of the Wild Frontier'
  • Scots-Irish family that migrated down the Appalachian frontier
  • Died defending the Alamo with some 200 others
Miles D. AndrossHalf 6th cousin 6× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Alamo defender Texas / Alamo Bradford, Vermontc. 1804 The Alamo, San Antonio, Texas6 Mar 1836
  • Defender of the Alamo — the only Vermonter to die there
  • Father and grandfather both served in the Continental forces of the Revolution
Capt. John H. ForsythHalf 7th cousin 4× removed Scots-Irish1717–1775 Alamo defender Texas / Alamo Avon, New York10 Aug 1797 The Alamo, San Antonio, Texas6 Mar 1836
  • Third-ranking officer at the Battle of the Alamo, commanding the cavalry company
  • Grandfather, of Scottish Clan Forsyth, died fighting in the American Revolution
  • Studied medicine before heading to Texas after his wife's death
The UK BranchBritain – Hollywood – Switzerland
Charlie ChaplinHalf 6th cousin 3× removed The UK Branch — — Walworth, London16 Apr 1889 Corsier-sur-Vevey, Switzerland25 Dec 1977
  • The branch that stayed in Britain — and then conquered Hollywood
  • Rose from South London poverty to become cinema's first global icon
  • Barred from re-entering the United States during the McCarthy era; knighted 1975
Folkways: The migration geographies ref: David Hackett Fischer, Albion's Seed
  • New England — East Anglian Puritans to Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, New Hampshire, Vermont & Maine.
  • Delaware Valley — Midlands Quakers to southeastern Pennsylvania, New Jersey & Delaware.
  • Tidewater — southern-English Cavaliers to the Chesapeake coastal plain: eastern Virginia, southern Maryland & northeastern North Carolina.
  • Backcountry — Ulster Scots and Borderers to the Appalachian frontier: western Pennsylvania, the Shenandoah, West Virginia, the Carolina uplands, east Tennessee, Kentucky & north Georgia.
